categories.title: Admin & Auth
categories.sub: Gates, shells, and the strategy docs behind them.

mechanics.name: PayNow QR Generator
mechanics.summary: Singapore QR code for instant bank-app payments.

details.plain: Generates a scannable QR code so Singapore customers can pay you directly from their bank app (DBS, UOB, OCBC, etc.). No credit-card fees, instant settlement.
details.when_use: Any site that takes SG payments: donations, checkout, invoicing, event tickets.
details.when_skip: International customers can't scan PayNow QRs — use Stripe or PayPal instead if your audience is outside Singapore.

mechanics.name: LocalStorage State
mechanics.summary: Saves user data in their browser so it survives page refresh.

details.plain: Stores data in the user's own browser — if they refresh the page or come back tomorrow, their stuff is still there. Think of it like the browser remembering what's in your shopping cart.
details.when_use: Cart contents, quiz progress, game save, editor drafts, filter preferences, onboarding state.
details.when_skip: Data you need across devices (laptop + phone) — that needs a real database. LocalStorage is browser-local only.

mechanics.name: Admin Auth Gate
mechanics.summary: Password screen that hides a page from casual visitors.

details.plain: Shows a password prompt before letting visitors see the page. Only people who know the password get in. It's the 'lock on the door' for internal pages.
details.when_use: Admin dashboards, internal tools, pre-release demos shared with trusted users, competitor-analytics panels.
details.when_skip: Real user accounts with sign-up flows — this is a shared-password gate (dev-grade), not a production auth system. Use Supabase or NextAuth for that.

mechanics.name: Canvas Hero
mechanics.summary: Moving background for your hero section (stars, particles, etc.).

details.plain: An animated background at the top of your page — drifting stars, flowing particles, geometric shapes. Makes the hero section feel alive instead of static.
details.when_use: Tech, gaming, defence, aviation, sci-fi brands — anywhere motion reinforces the vibe.
details.when_skip: Warm, editorial, healthcare, or minimalist brands. Motion can feel out of place and annoying. Also skip for users on slow devices — it costs CPU.

mechanics.name: Chart.js Dashboard
mechanics.summary: Turn numbers into line/bar/pie charts with a 'last updated' badge.

details.plain: Renders interactive graphs — line charts for trends, bar charts for comparisons, pie charts for proportions. Includes a 'Last updated X ago' badge so users can tell if the data is fresh or stale.
details.when_use: Dashboards, KPI widgets, analytics pages, simulator output, revenue/cashflow visuals.
details.when_skip: If you just need one tiny sparkline, Chart.js is overkill (270KB library). Use an inline SVG instead.

mechanics.name: PDF Pipeline
mechanics.summary: Turn any section of your page into a downloadable PDF.

details.plain: Captures any part of your webpage — charts, tables, forms, text — and packages it into a downloadable PDF. Handles multi-page reports automatically so content doesn't get cut off.
details.when_use: Report generators, resume exports, invoices, quotations, certificates, summary documents users want to save or print.
details.when_skip: If users just need to print, they can use browser Print (Ctrl+P). Also avoid if users need to copy text from the PDF — this method produces image-based PDFs.

mechanics.name: Wizard Form
mechanics.summary: Break a long form into small steps with a progress bar.

details.plain: Instead of overwhelming users with a wall of form fields, split it into small steps (Step 1 of 4, Step 2 of 4…) with a progress bar and Next/Back buttons. People finish long forms at higher rates this way.
details.when_use: Multi-step checkout, character creation, onboarding, quiz engines, diagnostics, application forms with 5+ fields.
details.when_skip: Short forms (2-3 fields) — wizards add friction where none was needed. Just show all fields at once.

mechanics.name: Multi-Page Scaffold
mechanics.summary: A standard nav bar + footer shown on every page automatically.

details.plain: A consistent top nav bar and footer that appears on every page of your site, automatically highlighting which page you're currently on. Includes a mobile hamburger menu for phones.
details.when_use: Any site with more than one page — marketing sites, multi-step stories, product catalogs with detail pages.
details.when_skip: Single-page sites (landing pages, one-shot calculators) — you don't need nav links to nowhere.

mechanics.name: OG / Social Meta
mechanics.summary: Makes your link show a nice preview when shared on WhatsApp, Twitter, etc.

details.plain: When someone pastes your site URL into WhatsApp, Twitter, Slack, iMessage, LinkedIn, or Discord, this is what makes a preview card appear — image + title + description — instead of a boring raw link. Gets 3-5× more clicks.
details.when_use: Every site, every page. Mandatory. Pairs with og-thumbnail (which designs the image itself).

mechanics.name: OG / WhatsApp Thumbnail
mechanics.summary: The 1200×630 image asset that powers WhatsApp / Twitter / iMessage link previews.

details.plain: The actual IMAGE file that sits behind your site's shared-link preview. Whereas og-social-meta is the <meta> tags, this mechanic covers how to DESIGN a good 1200×630 thumbnail: layout patterns, text treatment, visual anchors, brand expression, CTA placement. 7 mix-and-match factors in the README.
details.when_use: Every site. Pair with og-social-meta — the tags point to the image this produces.
details.when_skip: Never — a site without a shared-link preview looks broken on WhatsApp.

mechanics.name: Favicon Set
mechanics.summary: The little icon in every browser tab, bookmark, and phone home screen.

details.plain: The tiny icon users see in every browser tab, bookmark list, and phone home screen. A site without one feels unfinished — visitors notice the blank globe and trust drops. Design-at-16×16 is its own craft; picking the right shape/color/format strategy matters.
details.when_use: Every site, mandatory. Mix-and-match 5 factors (shape × color × format × theme × mobile-treatment) — README has 3 ready-made recipes.

mechanics.name: Admin Insights Panel
mechanics.summary: Persona cards + pricing grids + willingness-to-pay analysis for strategic business sites.

details.plain: A separate admin-side page (usually /admin-insights.html) that lays out the market thinking behind the public site: 2-4 target personas as cards, competitive-landscape tables, pricing-tier grids, willingness-to-pay analysis, and 'why this works' rationale. It's the internal strategy doc that shaped the public copy — kept alongside the site so you (and future Claude sessions) remember WHY the pricing and messaging say what they say.
details.when_use: Any site selling a service, product, or idea where pricing and positioning are non-obvious — B2B services, SaaS, EOR, clinics, marketplaces, resume tools.
details.when_skip: Pure informational sites, portfolios, or games — no pricing thinking to document. Also skip if the public site IS the strategy doc (e.g., pitch decks).

mechanics.name: Design Variant Switcher
mechanics.summary: Ship the same page in 3-9 aesthetic variants so the client can pick the winner.

details.plain: Instead of guessing which aesthetic the client will like, build the same page 3-9 times with different CSS-variable palettes (editorial, clinical, obsidian, twilight, cinematic…). Each variant is either a separate file (style-editorial.html, style-clinical.html…) or one file with data-theme swaps. Client picks one, you throw the rest away. The non-obvious skill is a disciplined CSS-variable architecture so only the :root block changes, not the markup.
details.when_use: Early client work where brand direction is unsettled — clinics, premium services, luxury brands, anywhere 'how does it feel?' matters more than 'what does it say?'.
details.when_skip: When brand guidelines are locked in, or the site is purely informational. Also skip if the client isn't available to pick — variants without a decision-maker just rot.

mechanics.name: Full-Dashboard Admin Shell
mechanics.summary: A whole admin app behind a login: sidebar nav, KPI cards, tables, modals — not just a password prompt.

details.plain: The whole admin scaffold: login gate → sidebar nav with 4-8 sections → top KPI row → data tables with pills/badges → modals for edit flows → localStorage for session + draft state. Distinct from admin-auth-gate (which is just the password). Reach for this when the admin page is itself an application — 5+ sections, real CRUD, not a single internal report.
details.when_use: Lead-gen ops dashboards, event management, internal CRMs, sales pipelines, project management — any internal tool that's a full app.
details.when_skip: If the admin page is one table or one report, admin-auth-gate + a single page is enough. This shell is overkill for single-purpose internal pages.

mechanics.name: Streamlit Analytics Scaffold
mechanics.summary: Full Python analytics engine: data → cache → compute → render → theme, with guest rate limiting.

details.plain: When HTML + Chart.js stops being enough (real math, live APIs, big datasets, PDF exports), Streamlit is the escape hatch. Covers the whole stack: data layer (yfinance / REST), st.cache_data for compute, Plotly for charts, custom CSS for theme polish, an IP-based guest-limit module for daily usage quotas, weasyprint for PDF reports, deployed to Streamlit Community Cloud. Battle-tested on one deep build (market-tracker) — documented here so the next analytics project doesn't rediscover the stack.
details.when_use: Finance, analytics, research tools that do real computation — portfolio optimization, Monte Carlo, ML inference, anything you'd otherwise fight JavaScript to deliver.
details.when_skip: Anything that's fundamentally a marketing or content site. Streamlit styling is rigid — don't use it when design polish matters more than data power.

mechanics.name: Formspree Form
mechanics.summary: Wire a static HTML form to an email inbox without any backend.

details.plain: Turns a plain <form action="https://formspree.io/f/YOUR_ID"> into a working email-capture endpoint — no backend, no server, no API keys in the browser. Submissions land in your inbox (free tier: 50/month). Works beautifully with GitHub Pages where you can't run a backend. Pair with a WhatsApp CTA for a hybrid capture flow — Formspree as the 'leave your details' path, WhatsApp as the 'talk to me now' path.
details.when_use: Lead-gen diagnostics, contact forms, RSVP, newsletter signup, bug reports — any static site that needs to capture structured input and email it to you.
details.when_skip: If submissions need to land in a database, trigger workflows, or show up in an admin dashboard — use Supabase or Airtable. Also skip once you'll exceed 50/mo consistently; at that volume Supabase's free tier is cheaper.
